name: Trampolining

x.formalRole: Reifies recursion as data so a flat loop ("trampoline") drives it, converting stack recursion into heap-bounded iteration.
x.compositionRule: A step returns either Done(value) or More(thunk); the trampoline loops, calling thunks until Done.
laws: Preserves the semantics of the original recursion; only the evaluation strategy changes
x.encoding: A sum type Done|More plus a driver loop. Manual in most languages; needed where there is no tail-call optimisation.
x.useCases: Deep / mutual recursion in languages without TCO (Java, C#) to avoid stack overflow.
misuseRisks: Adding trampolines where TCO or a plain loop already works
Allocation overhead from thunks in hot paths

def: A technique that turns deep or mutual recursion into a heap-driven loop, avoiding stack overflow in languages without guaranteed tail-call optimisation.
problem: Recursive (especially mutually-recursive) algorithms blow the call stack where the runtime does not eliminate tail calls.
mechanism: Each recursive step returns a value (Done) or a thunk describing the next step (More) instead of calling itself. A driver loop repeatedly invokes returned thunks, keeping stack depth constant at the cost of heap thunks.
benefits: Constant stack usage for deep recursion
limits: Slower than native TCO when available
failures: Forgetting to return More (re-introducing stack growth)
Using in hot loops where allocation dominates
whenNot: Languages/compilers with guaranteed TCO (use tail recursion)
myths: "Functional code can’t overflow the stack" — without TCO it can; trampolines fix it
